verses.text: And it came to pass, when he had made an end of speaking unto Saul, that the soul of Jonathan was knit with the soul of David, and Jonathan loved him as his own soul. And Saul took him that day, and would let him go no more home to his father's house. And Jonathan and David made a covenant, because he loved him as his own soul. And Jonathan stripped himself of the robe that was upon him, and gave it to David, and his garments, even to his sword, and to his bow, and to his girdle. Guidance: 'The soul of Jonathan was knit with the soul of David' — the verb (qashar) is the same one used of a strong cord tying two things into one. Jonathan, the crown prince of Israel, watches a shepherd boy speak with God-confidence to his father, and his heart binds to David's heart in a friendship that will define both their lives. Saul detains David at court the same day (the harp-playing visits from chapter 16 now become permanent residence). And Jonathan does something only he could have done: he strips off his royal robe, his armor, his sword, bow, and belt, and gives them all to David. This is not a teenage giveaway; this is the heir to the throne handing the regalia of succession to the man God has chosen. Jonathan does it without knowing what God has done at Bethlehem (16:13), but the gesture is providentially exact. Jonathan is one of the most beautiful figures in scripture precisely because he can give up what others would kill to keep — even to one whom he could easily have read as his rival. Friendship rooted in shared theology can choose another's elevation over one's own inheritance without losing anything that mattered.

And David went out whithersoever Saul sent him, and behaved himself wisely: and Saul set him over the men of war, and he was accepted in the sight of all the people, and also in the sight of Saul's servants. Guidance: A single summary verse for David's early military career under Saul. The verb 'behaved himself wisely' (sakal) is used four times of David in this chapter (v5, v14, v15, v30) — it is the defining word for his bearing. It means more than 'acted shrewdly'; it implies a Spirit-given competence that makes his actions land successfully and well-received. Three audiences sign off on him: 'all the people' (the rank-and-file militia), 'Saul's servants' (the court), and Saul himself (who promotes him). David is universally well-regarded. Notice the order in which the chapter will sequence: this is the high point of David's relationship with everyone, including Saul, including the kingdom. From here on, the chapter will track Saul's hatred souring against an uncomplicated rise. The most dangerous people sometimes are those who first promoted you, when your continued rise begins to make their place feel small.

And it came to pass as they came, when David was returned from the slaughter of the Philistine, that the women came out of all cities of Israel, singing and dancing, to meet king Saul, with tabrets, with joy, and with instruments of musick. And the women answered one another as they played, and said, Saul hath slain his thousands, and David his ten thousands. Guidance: The famous victory song. Israelite women traditionally welcomed the returning army with timbrels, dancing, and antiphonal singing (Miriam at the Red Sea, Deborah after Sisera, Jephthah's daughter). The two-part chorus they invent here — 'Saul hath slain his thousands, and David his ten thousands' — is a piece of standard Hebrew parallelism in which the second line raises the numbers of the first not as literal demographics but as poetic intensification. (Hebrew couplets typically heighten in the second half; 'thousands' and 'ten thousands' is a standard intensification pair, not a careful body-count.) In Israelite poetic convention, the song honored both kings; in Saul's hearing, it humiliated him. The same song, heard by different ears, can sound very different — and the ear that hears praise-of-another as insult-of-self has already begun to rot.

(Hebrew couplets typically heighten in the second half; 'thousands' and 'ten thousands' is a standard intensification pair, not a careful body-count.) And Saul was very wroth, and the saying displeased him; and he said, They have ascribed unto David ten thousands, and to me they have ascribed but thousands: and what can he have more but the kingdom? And Saul eyed David from that day and forward. Guidance: Saul does the math in private — and the math is no longer arithmetic; it is paranoia. 'What can he have more but the kingdom?' He hears in the women's song a prophecy he was never told but which is in fact accurate (Samuel had already promised the kingdom to a 'neighbour better than thou,' 15:28). The terrible irony: Saul, who never let himself believe Samuel's verdict, now arrives at the same conclusion through jealousy. He has now identified the rival, and 'Saul eyed David from that day and forward' — the Hebrew 'avan ('eyed') is the same root as 'envy / regard suspiciously.' From this verse to Saul's death, the king will watch David through a poisoned lens. Sin's progress is observable in the verbs of the eye: at first he loved David greatly (16:21); now he merely eyes him; soon he will hate him; eventually he will hunt him. Every step of the spiral started with reframing a victory song.

And it came to pass on the morrow, that the evil spirit from God came upon Saul, and he prophesied in the midst of the house: and David played with his hand, as at other times: and there was a javelin in Saul's hand. And Saul cast the javelin; for he said, I will smite David even to the wall with it. And David avoided out of his presence twice. Guidance: 'On the morrow' — the very next day. Resentment fermented overnight becomes attempted murder by morning. The tormenting spirit comes upon Saul; he 'prophesied' — the Hebrew can mean ecstatic speech of any kind, not necessarily true prophecy; here it is probably wild incoherent ranting that the king's family must endure. David, still loyal to the music ministry that has soothed Saul before, picks up the harp and plays. Saul, javelin in hand (an aristocrat's standing accessory, like a sword), suddenly hurls it at David, intending to pin him to the wall. The detail 'as at other times' is heartbreaking — the very routine of healing has become the moment of attempted assassination. David 'avoided out of his presence twice' — Hebrew implies he dodged twice and ducked the spear two separate times, which suggests Saul threw twice. David does not retaliate, does not even leave the room permanently; he simply evades each throw and stays in service. Loving someone who is dangerous to you does not always require leaving; sometimes it requires staying with quicker reflexes. But the line has been crossed; David now knows Saul wants him dead.

And Saul was afraid of David, because the LORD was with him, and was departed from Saul. Therefore Saul removed him from him, and made him his captain over a thousand; and he went out and came in before the people. Guidance: Saul, having failed to spear David, takes a different tack. He demotes-by-promotion: makes him captain over a thousand and sends him out from the inner court to the field. The reason given is the deepest one: 'Saul was afraid of David, because the LORD was with him, and was departed from Saul.' The whole tragedy in one sentence. The presence-asymmetry — David Spirit-filled, Saul Spirit-deserted — is what produces Saul's fear. He cannot bear David's proximity because David's presence is a daily exposure of Saul's absence. The plan is also tactical: a field commander leading a thousand into combat is statistically likely to die. Saul is hoping casualty-rate will achieve what the spear could not. 'He went out and came in before the people' — David accepts the demotion with no protest and turns it into more wide visibility before the men. The very mechanism Saul devised to remove David from court instead puts David in front of every soldier in the levy. God uses Saul's strategy against itself.

And David behaved himself wisely in all his ways; and the LORD was with him. And Saul saw that he behaved himself very wisely, he was afraid of him. But all Israel and Judah loved David, because he went out and came in before them. Guidance: The narrator repeats the verb three times in this section — 'behaved himself wisely… very wisely.' David's competence is not eroded by Saul's hostility; it is sharpened by it. The hostility itself becomes Saul's mirror: 'Saul saw he behaved himself very wisely, he was afraid of him.' Saul has now reached the inverse position from a healthy ruler — instead of being reassured by competence in his lieutenants, he is alarmed by it. And the wider audience continues to widen: 'all Israel and Judah loved David.' Notice the split between Israel and Judah being named separately for the second time in 1 Samuel — the kingdom that will split a few generations later is already named in two halves by the narrator. David is loved on both sides of the eventual split. The future king's kingdom-wide affection is already building, while Saul's tightens to a court of suspicion.

And Saul said to David, Behold my elder daughter Merab, her will I give thee to wife: only be thou valiant for me, and fight the LORD's battles. For Saul said, Let not mine hand be upon him, but let the hand of the Philistines be upon him. And David said unto Saul, Who am I? and what is my life, or my father's family in Israel, that I should be son in law to the king? But it came to pass at the time when Merab Saul's daughter should have been given to David, that she was given unto Adriel the Meholathite to wife. Guidance: Saul tries a second variation on the same death-by-Philistine theme: offer David his older daughter Merab in marriage, on condition that he keep fighting Israel's battles. The narrator gives us the king's inner thought verbatim: 'let not mine hand be upon him, but let the hand of the Philistines be upon him.' Saul has now decided to outsource the assassination to the enemy. He frames this as a generous royal gesture; he means it as bait. David's reply is exquisite: 'Who am I? what is my life, or my father's family in Israel, that I should be son in law to the king?' He responds with humility — the family of a shepherd from Bethlehem cannot be presumed worthy of royal marriage. There may be social calculation here too (the bride-price of a king's daughter would be huge), but the dominant note is the same modesty that runs through David's psalms: 'who am I?' And then the cruelest twist: when the wedding day actually arrives, Saul gives Merab to someone else entirely — Adriel the Meholathite — without informing David. This is not just a withdrawn promise; it is a public humiliation, broadcast at the moment of greatest exposure. Saul is now in the business of breaking his royal word to hurt David's standing. The line between bad-faith ruler and tyrant has been crossed.

And Michal Saul's daughter loved David: and they told Saul, and the thing pleased him. And Saul said, I will give him her, that she may be a snare to him, and that the hand of the Philistines may be against him. Wherefore Saul said to David, Thou shalt this day be my son in law in the one of the twain. Guidance: Younger daughter Michal develops a real love for David. (Michal is the only woman in the Hebrew Bible whom the text explicitly says loved a man, rather than the other way around.) When word reaches Saul, he is pleased — but not in fatherly delight. The narrator unmasks the calculation: 'I will give him her, that she may be a snare to him, and that the hand of the Philistines may be against him.' Saul is recycling the Merab plan with a daughter whose love can be weaponized. The Hebrew 'snare' (moqesh) is the trap a hunter sets — Saul is treating his daughter's affection as bait. 'Thou shalt this day be my son in law in the one of the twain' — that is, in the second of the two daughters. Saul tries again with the same scheme. The chapter is making us watch a father use his daughter's heart as a murder instrument; few biblical scenes are more morally ugly. Michal will love David through the next chapter's window escape, then be torn from him by her father, given to Phaltiel, and eventually returned to David in cold political brokerage. The wreckage of Saul's house begins with this conversation.

And Saul commanded his servants, saying, Commune with David secretly, and say, Behold, the king hath delight in thee, and all his servants love thee: now therefore be the king's son in law. And Saul's servants spake those words in the ears of David. And David said, Seemeth it to you a light thing to be a king's son in law, seeing that I am a poor man, and lightly esteemed? Guidance: Saul does not even speak the offer himself; he sends servants to communicate it 'secretly,' as if the king were too modest to press the matter directly. The script he gives them is flattering: 'the king hath delight in thee, and all his servants love thee.' Half-true (the servants probably do love David; the king does not). David's reply is again notable. 'Seemeth it to you a light thing to be a king's son in law, seeing that I am a poor man, and lightly esteemed?' The poverty is real (a shepherd family from Bethlehem could not pay a royal bride-price). The 'lightly esteemed' (qalah) is also a real social fact, though less true week-by-week — David's standing is rising — but the disposition remains modest. David's hesitation is not coy; he is genuinely floored by the gap between his station and the throne. This is the same young man who said 'who am I?' and the same heart that will later say in Psalm 8 'what is man, that thou art mindful of him?' The royal sonship offered to him is recognized as out of proportion to his place. The right disposition before kings is also the right disposition before God.

And the servants of Saul told him, saying, On this manner spake David. And Saul said, Thus shall ye say to David, The king desireth not any dowry, but an hundred foreskins of the Philistines, to be avenged of the king's enemies. But Saul thought to make David fall by the hand of the Philistines. Guidance: Saul sees the opening David's modesty gives him and exploits it brutally. 'The king desireth not any dowry, but an hundred foreskins of the Philistines.' Marriage gifts (mohar) were normally paid in silver or livestock; substituting a war trophy was unheard of, and the specific trophy — circumcision is the covenant mark on Israelite males; the Philistines, being uncircumcised, would have to be killed and circumcised post-mortem to provide the trophy — was designed to require deep penetration into hostile territory. The narrator gives Saul's intent plainly: 'Saul thought to make David fall by the hand of the Philistines.' One hundred guerrilla strikes on patrols deep behind Philistine lines was a statistical death sentence. The request was meant to be the funeral notice. Few moments in Saul's life expose his moral corruption more starkly than this — using the covenant sign of Israel as the criterion for the bride-price he intended to use to kill the bridegroom-elect. Religion and murder, woven together, has been Saul's signature since chapter 14.

And the servants of Saul told him, saying, On this manner spake David. And Saul said, Thus shall ye say to David, The king desireth not any dowry, but an hundred foreskins of the Philistines, to be avenged of the king's enemies. But Saul thought to make David fall by the hand of the Philistines. Guidance: Saul sees the opening David's modesty gives him and exploits it brutally. 'The king desireth not any dowry, but an hundred foreskins of the Philistines.' Marriage gifts (mohar) were normally paid in silver or livestock; substituting a war trophy was unheard of, and the specific trophy — circumcision is the covenant mark on Israelite males; the Philistines, being uncircumcised, would have to be killed and circumcised post-mortem to provide the trophy — was designed to require deep penetration into hostile territory. The narrator gives Saul's intent plainly: 'Saul thought to make David fall by the hand of the Philistines.' One hundred guerrilla strikes on patrols deep behind Philistine lines was a statistical death sentence. The request was meant to be the funeral notice. Few moments in Saul's life expose his moral corruption more starkly than this — using the covenant sign of Israel as the criterion for the bride-price he intended to use to kill the bridegroom-elect. Religion and murder, woven together, has been Saul's signature since chapter 14.

And when his servants told David these words, it pleased David well to be the king's son in law: and the days were not expired. Wherefore David arose and went, he and his men, and slew of the Philistines two hundred men; and David brought their foreskins, and they gave them in full tale to the king, that he might be the king's son in law. And Saul gave him Michal his daughter to wife. Guidance: David accepts the impossible bride-price. 'It pleased David well' — the Hebrew implies an eager assent. And then, the doubling: 'David brought two hundred.' Saul asked for one hundred; David delivered double. The number is not bravado but providence — David has more than fulfilled the condition, and Saul cannot wriggle out of giving Michal to him as he did with Merab. There is also a quiet detail: 'he and his men' — David already has a small fighting unit under him. The men who will follow him into Adullam and the wilderness years are already forming a tight loyal nucleus. They follow him deep into Philistine territory on what should have been a suicide mission, and they all come back with the trophies. 'In full tale' (b'milletum) means 'counted out in full' — Saul has to publicly verify the count. 'Saul gave him Michal his daughter to wife.' The king's hand is forced; the marriage Saul tried to weaponize becomes the protective place from which David will escape Saul's first night assassination attempt in chapter 19. God turns every bullet Saul aims at David into a piece of David's armor.

And when his servants told David these words, it pleased David well to be the king's son in law: and the days were not expired. Wherefore David arose and went, he and his men, and slew of the Philistines two hundred men; and David brought their foreskins, and they gave them in full tale to the king, that he might be the king's son in law. And Saul gave him Michal his daughter to wife. Guidance: David accepts the impossible bride-price. 'It pleased David well' — the Hebrew implies an eager assent. And then, the doubling: 'David brought two hundred.' Saul asked for one hundred; David delivered double. The number is not bravado but providence — David has more than fulfilled the condition, and Saul cannot wriggle out of giving Michal to him as he did with Merab. There is also a quiet detail: 'he and his men' — David already has a small fighting unit under him. The men who will follow him into Adullam and the wilderness years are already forming a tight loyal nucleus. They follow him deep into Philistine territory on what should have been a suicide mission, and they all come back with the trophies. 'In full tale' (b'milletum) means 'counted out in full' — Saul has to publicly verify the count. 'Saul gave him Michal his daughter to wife.' The king's hand is forced; the marriage Saul tried to weaponize becomes the protective place from which David will escape Saul's first night assassination attempt in chapter 19. God turns every bullet Saul aims at David into a piece of David's armor.

And Saul saw and knew that the LORD was with David, and that Michal Saul's daughter loved him. And Saul was yet the more afraid of David; and Saul became David's enemy continually. Guidance: 'Saul saw and knew that the LORD was with David.' The verb 'knew' (yada) is full knowledge, not suspicion. Saul is not merely jealous; he is theologically informed. He recognizes the same Spirit-presence on David that he himself once had and lost. And the response to clear theological recognition is not repentance but worse fear. The man who diagnosed God's hand on his rival could have repented; instead he gripped harder. Verse 29 names the new permanent state: 'Saul became David's enemy continually.' The Hebrew 'continually' (kol-hayamim) is 'all the days' — the rest of Saul's life will be one long pursuit. The relationship is finalized. From here onward, every chapter is the king of Israel hunting the LORD's anointed across the wilderness. The chapter that opened with the most beautiful friendship in scripture (Jonathan) ends with the most relentless enmity (Saul). The same David elicits both responses, and the difference is not in David but in the two hearts looking at him.

The Hebrew 'continually' (kol-hayamim) is 'all the days' — the rest of Saul's life will be one long pursuit. The relationship is finalized. Then the princes of the Philistines went forth: and it came to pass, after they went forth, that David behaved himself more wisely than all the servants of Saul; so that his name was much set by. Guidance: A summary verse. The Philistine commanders ('princes' is the technical title for the rulers of the five Philistine cities, the seranim) keep raiding into Israel; David keeps fighting them; and 'David behaved himself more wisely than all the servants of Saul.' That word for the fourth time in this chapter — sakal, the Spirit-given competence. David is now outperforming the entire court. 'His name was much set by' — yiqar shemo, 'his name became precious / weighty / honored.' The king who wanted David's name forgotten has instead presided over its rise to where it eclipses every other officer's. The narrative arc of the chapter is complete: the harpist becomes the public hero, the family-promised becomes the king's son-in-law, the hunted-by-spear walks out under a name more honored than all of Saul's officials combined. God's hand is unbearable to the man fighting it and unstoppable for the man it rests on. The book is now set for chapters 19-31 — the long wilderness contest of the rejected king against the chosen one. Hold the picture: the chapter has prepared every piece on the board.
